indicators.name: Total postsecondary enrollment
indicators.description: Headline signal for whether the demographic cliff is showing up in actual enrollment numbers yet.

current.note: Undergraduate enrollment (+1.3%) is driving overall growth, led by community colleges and certificate programs; graduate enrollment essentially flat (-0.1%).

indicators.name: High school graduate pipeline
indicators.source: WICHE Knocking at the College Door
indicators.description: Leading indicator (3-5 yr out) for the size of the future applicant pool.

thresholds.label: Worsening
thresholds.band: Revised projections steeper decline than prior forecast

current.note: No new edition published since; national peak of ~3.9M graduates in 2025 confirmed, decline through 2041 remains the operative forecast.

indicators.name: FAFSA completion rate
indicators.description: Near-term leading indicator of next fall's enrollment, especially for access-sensitive institutions.

current.note: Up from 53.9% for the class of 2025 -- about 5 points ahead, driven by an early FAFSA launch and smoother processing.

trends.title: Alternative Credentials Are Displacing the Degree

indicators.name: Non-degree vs. degree completions
indicators.description: Whether certificates/badges are growing faster than degree completions.

thresholds.label: Accelerating
thresholds.band: Non-degree growth outpaces degree growth by > 3x

current.note: Certificate completions hit a record 525,200, growing 11% while bachelor's and associate completions both declined -- clearest sign yet of workforce-aligned credential substitution.

indicators.name: Employer degree-requirement removal rate
indicators.source: LinkedIn Economic Graph / Indeed Hiring Lab
indicators.description: Demand-side validation that employers are actually dropping degree requirements.

thresholds.label: Accelerating
thresholds.band: >1 ppt/quarter increase in postings without degree requirement

current.value: Composition-adjusted bachelor's-requirement share ticked up to 18.6%
current.note: After hitting a 5-year low of 18.5% in March 2024, the share of postings requiring a degree (adjusted for job mix) has been trending slightly upward for two years -- policy announcements are outpacing actual hiring-practice change.

indicators.name: Institutional AI/credential adoption
indicators.description: Whether institutions are building AI-related credentials and adapting curricula.

thresholds.label: Accelerating
thresholds.band: Majority of institutions report new AI-related credentials

current.value: 95% now have a work-related AI strategy; 64% report 5+ strategy elements
current.as_of: January 2026 (EDUCAUSE 'Impact of AI on Work in Higher Education')
current.note: Institutional caution is fading too -- only 15% describe leadership as cautious about AI, down from 20-23% in 2024-25.

indicators.name: Enterprises with AI in production
indicators.source: McKinsey State of AI annual survey
indicators.description: Gap between AI experimentation and real production deployment.

thresholds.label: Accelerating
thresholds.band: >10 ppt YoY increase in production deployment

current.value: 88% regularly use AI in at least one function
current.note: Up from a mix of narrower gen-AI-only figures around 71-78% a year earlier, though McKinsey notes nearly two-thirds still haven't begun enterprise-wide scaling.

indicators.name: AI-related job postings share
indicators.source: LinkedIn Economic Graph / Indeed Hiring Lab
indicators.description: Real-time labor-market demand signal for AI skills.

current.value: Indeed AI Tracker hit 4.2% of postings
current.note: AI-mentioning postings surged over 130% in 2025 while total postings grew only ~6% above pre-pandemic baseline -- a clear divergence.

indicators.name: Hyperscaler AI capex
indicators.source: Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta quarterly earnings
indicators.description: Whether infrastructure investment still supports the demand narrative.

current.note: Alphabet and Meta both raised guidance mid-year; Microsoft's $190B and Amazon's ~$200B came in above analyst estimates.

indicators.name: Tech layoffs volume
indicators.description: Direct measure of workforce contraction in the sector.

current.note: Averaging well above 20,000/month; May 2026 was the highest single month in years, with AI cited as the leading driver in 56% of 2026 layoff events.

indicators.name: Tech/information sector unemployment rate
indicators.description: Whether layoffs are translating into sustained unemployment or fast re-absorption.

current.value: Overall unemployment 4.2%; Information sector -9,000 jobs, Professional/Business Services +36,000
current.note: Mixed by subsector -- Information is shedding jobs but Professional & Business Services overall is still adding, keeping the headline rate stable.

indicators.name: AI regulation activity
indicators.source: IAPP / NCSL / MultiState AI legislation trackers
indicators.description: Regulatory overhead that could shape hiring, compliance costs, and product velocity.

thresholds.label: Accelerating
thresholds.band: New enacted state/federal AI laws this quarter

current.value: 1,561 state AI bills introduced by March 2026; new laws enacted in CO, CA, NY, CT
current.note: Colorado's comprehensive AI Act (SB 205) takes effect June 30, 2026; California, New York, and Connecticut all enacted algorithmic-pricing laws this year. No federal AI law has advanced.

indicators.name: Share of postings dropping degree requirements
indicators.source: LinkedIn Economic Graph / Indeed Hiring Lab
indicators.description: Labor-market side view that cross-validates the Higher Ed credential-substitution trend.

current.value: Same data as He-5: composition-adjusted share up to 18.6%
current.note: Employer survey commitments (23% report removing requirements per Lumina-Gallup) haven't yet shown up in the composition-adjusted posting data.

indicators.name: Skills-based hiring adoption rate
indicators.description: Whether employers report actually changing hiring practice, not just posting language.

current.note: Could not confirm a 2026 LinkedIn Talent Solutions adoption-rate figure in this run -- related research (Harvard/Burning Glass, OneTen) suggests actual hiring-pattern change is lagging stated commitments, but no clean YoY adoption number was found. Needs a targeted follow-up search.

indicators.name: WEF net displacement forecast
indicators.source: World Economic Forum Future of Jobs Report
indicators.description: Structural, long-horizon signal on net job creation vs. displacement from automation/AI.

current.value: 2025 report still the operative forecast; interim LinkedIn data shows +1.3M AI-linked jobs globally
current.as_of: WEF Future of Jobs 2025; LinkedIn interim data January 2026
current.note: No new WEF edition yet (next full report expected 2027). Interim LinkedIn data suggests AI is a net job creator so far, not the net-displacement scenario some feared -- consistent with, not worse than, the 2025 forecast.

trends.title: Hybrid Work Norm Solidifies, RTO Mandates Plateau

indicators.name: Return-to-office mandate tracker
indicators.description: Whether major employers are still tightening in-office requirements or have plateaued.

current.value: 34% of US firms now fully in-office, up from 27% at end of 2025
current.note: Continued wave of mandates (Home Depot, Stellantis, Microsoft phased return) -- still a minority position, but growing quarter over quarter.

indicators.name: Remote job postings share
indicators.description: Direct measure of remote-eligible hiring supply.

current.note: Did not find a clean, current Ladders/FlexJobs posting-share figure in this run. Related BLS/Flex Index data shows overall remote work participation (22.6% in March 2026) essentially flat versus prior year -- suggestive of 'Stable' but not a direct match to the named source. Needs a targeted follow-up search.

indicators.name: Overall quits rate (JOLTS)
indicators.description: General labor market confidence/mobility context for RTO leverage.

current.note: Below the 'on track' band -- workers are moving jobs less, consistent with reduced leverage to resist RTO mandates.
